notes: "Pahoran's letter was full of reassurances to Moroni. It rebuilt the confidence he had had in the Governor of the Land, which in Moroni had been shattered, and replaced the parts thereof, like those of a cross-word puzzle, into a picture of joy and thanksgiving. Great was Moroni's rejoicing and unlimited the gladness of his heart when he knew that Pahoran had remained faithful, and had not, like many others, 'become a traitor to the freedom and cause of his country. The Sacred Record says of Moroni, 'His heart did take courage.' Somehow, someway, he seemed to think, 'All is not lost, the Lord God will, as in times past, come to our aid, and will deliver us from our enemies.' "Moroni's rejoicing was marred only by his sorrow for those who had driven Pahoran from the Judgment-Seat, also for those who for personal reasons and in pursuit of their own happiness had raised
country; yea, and whosoever was found denying their freedom was speedily executed according to the law.

quotes.text: Six thousand men are sent to Helaman. This turn of events rendered it easier for the restored government to meet the legitimate demands of the army, therefore, Moroni, its Commander-in-Chief, "immediately caused that provisions should be sent, and also an army of six thousand men should be sent unto Helaman, to assist him in preserving that part of the land." The same number of men together with an adequate supply of provisions were speedily dispatched to the army in the northeast under command of Lehi and Teancum. Moroni thought this latter of supreme importance because the area in which they were stationed was the key to the vast regions beyond, and to fortify it would be a deterrent to any invasion the Lamanites might attempt.
quotes.speaker: George Reynolds and Janne M. Sjodahl
quotes.source: Commentary on the Book of Mormon
quotes.attr: George Reynolds and Janne M. Sjodahl, Commentary on the Book of Mormon, edited and arranged by Philip C. Reynolds, 7 vols., 5:, p.180

notes: 14-17 Moroni and Pahoran with a large body of men took their march towards the Land of Nephihah. Notwithstanding the sudden call for help from Pahoran, to which Moroni was compelled to contribute. He continued to plan the deliverance of Nephihah from the Lamanitish hands, which was the thing he wanted most to do. But to his honor and perspicacity, he put that aside, and brought peace once more to Zarahemla. To do so was to him a diversionary movement, for it confronted his foes with a new problem, the manner of his approach. That diversion proved the strength of his leadership, and now he took up again the consideration of ways and means to liberate the City of Nephihah. In furtherance of the plans Moroni made, together with Pahoran, he left a large company of men to defend Zarahemla, and took with him another corps of troops and marched towards Nephihah "being determined to overthrow the Lamanites in that city." Providence lent a helping hand in their undertaking. As they followed a course that would lead them to

27-29 Many of the prisoners were desirous to join the people of Ammon. Among the prisoners taken by the Nephites were many Lamanites who desired to unite themselves with the Ammonites of whom they had heard, and with them become a free people. This was a like request as was made by a large number of Lamanites who also were captured by Moroni's army as they, that is, Moroni's army, were marching from Zarahemla to the relief of the City of Nephihah. The prisoners that were captured at both these times were granted like permission to join their former countrymen, and in doing so relieved the Nephites of a "great burthen" (same as burden), "yea," the Sacred Record says, "insomuch that they were relieved from all the prisoners of the Lamanites." Not only were the Nephites freed from the burden of caring for them, or, as we might say, "the burdensome sense of their responsibility," but the permission granted to the Lamanite prisoners by Moroni for them to join the People of Ammon made of them an industrious and prosperous segment of that people, instead of being a small portion of a loathsome and wicked race who delighted in murder and pillage. Again, we repeat the Book of Mormon: "Therefore, all the prisoners of the Lamanites did join the People of Ammon, and did begin to labor exceedingly tilling the ground, raising all manner of grain, and flocks and herds of every kind." After their defeat at Nephihah, the Lamanites began what may be termed a disorderly retreat southward. The battle for Nephihah ended in a decisive victory for Moroni's army. The great number of prisoners that was taken by the Nephites diminished exceedingly the strength of the Lamanites, while at the same time many Nephites who were held as prisoners by the Lamanites were released and joined the forces of Moroni, thus adding many veterans to his rapidly growing army. Moroni, sensing victory, gave the Lamanites no time to repair what proved to be disasterous for them. He followed their debacle, which was rendered more so because the Lamanites again fled from before his approaching army. It was not long before Lehi and Teancum with their army of tried veterans came into action, but as in the case of Moroni's troops, the Lamanites fled before them. From city to city the Lamanites raced to keep ahead of Moroni's men, until at last they all were gathered together near the City of Moroni which was close by the sea. Here was the scene of the final battle of the war. The Lamanites had concentrated into one body of do-or-die soldiers. The Nephites under the able leadership of Moroni, Lehi, and Teancum, pitched their tents in a circle around the bewildered Lamanites, save it was the wilderness side to the south and east. At their head, commanding the soldiers of Ammoron, was Ammoron, himself. Both the

quotes.text: Nephites and their foes were ready for a final and desperate stand, except for the fatigue caused by the previous days marches. The battle loomed up for the morrow.
quotes.speaker: George Reynolds and Janne M. Sjodahl
quotes.source: Commentary on the Book of Mormon
quotes.attr: George Reynolds and Janne M. Sjodahl, Commentary on the Book of Mormon, edited and arranged by Philip C. Reynolds, 7 vols., 5:, p.180

notes: that he considered that Ammoron, and Amalickiah his brother, had been the cause of this great and lasting war between them and the Lamanites, which had been the cause of so much war and bloodshed, yea, and so much famine.

Yet there stands the devil, the destroyer of this love, replacing it with anger and hostility. [When real suffering occurs in life, the devil is always there to sour men's hearts in anger, while the Lord continually emanates love.] In the same suffering "many had become hardened … and many were softened because of their afflictions" (Alma 62:41). What a wonderful example of how to respond to affliction. My friend William felt that way-hostile. It seemed that whatever happened, it was the Lord's fault-an illness, a death, a wayward child, a personal weakness, an "unanswered" prayer-all of which hardened his heart. His inner anger, which could flare up in but a moment, was directed toward God, his fellowman, and himself. From his heart emanated unbelief, stubbornness, pride, contention, and a loss of hope, love, and direction. He was miserable! (Gene R. Cook, in Conference Report, Apr. 2002, 97; or Ensign, May 2002, 82).

quotes.text: How many things there are in this life that appear to be one way and all of a sudden are the reverse. (See Alma 62:41.) Satan operates that way. He is the master of illusion. He creates illusions in an attempt to detour, dilute, and divert the power and the attention of the Latter-day Saints from the pure truth of God.
quotes.speaker: Gene R. Cook
quotes.attr: Gene R. Cook, in Conference Report, Apr. 1982, 36; or Ensign, May 1982, 25

quotes.text: Surely you know some whose lives have been filled with adversity who have been mellowed and strengthened and refined by it, while others have come away from the same test bitter and blistered and unhappy.
quotes.speaker: Boyd K. Packer
quotes.attr: Boyd K. Packer, in Conference Report, Oct. 1983, 22; or Ensign, Nov. 1983, 18

quotes.text: Being human, we would expel from our lives physical pain and mental anguish and assure ourselves of continual ease and comfort, but if we were to close the doors upon such sorrow and distress, we might be excluding our greatest friends and benefactors. Suffering can make saints of people as they learn patience, long-suffering, and self-mastery. The sufferings of our Savior were part of his education.
quotes.speaker: Teachings of Spencer W. Kimball

notes: "The long-continued and savage war just closed had brought various evils to the Church. In many parts of the land it may be said to have been disorganized. The occupancy of so many of the Nephite cities by the unbelieving Lamanites had produced numerous demoralizing effects. Murders, contentions, dissensions, and all manner of iniquity had become rife, and the hearts of many of the people had become hardened. Yet not altogether so, for there were some who acknowledged the hand of the Lord in their afflictions. These humbled themselves in the depths of humility; and because of the prayers of the righteous ones the people were spared. "Such was the state of affairs when Helaman went forth to call the people to repentance, and set the Church in order. In this blessed work he had much success, and with the help of his brethren he again
